Hey James, thanks for the response.  The problem is that I don't think I have any control over how those parameters are passed by the Cognito Login Page UI.  When you set that up in AWS they don't let you choose how to pass the parameters.  You can choose to use "token" or "code" for the login page which dictates which parameters are passed, but not how to pass them.  That seems to be a hard coded thing on the AWS side.  See: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/cognito-user-pools-app-ui-customization.html

I'm trying to use the AWS hosted login page to keep things as simple as possible for my web application.  This removes a lot of user management complexity from my source code.

The work around I'm going to use for now will mean implementing this flow:

1. React app route to serve as the callback handler from Cognito's login page
2. React app passes AWS JWT to a chalice managed handler which will validate it, create an application user if necessary, and return an application specific JWT back to the frontend to be used in future API calls.
3. If the JWT expires, redirect the user back to the AWS login page

I think that will work.  I plan to try and test that out this week.  Glad I'm not going crazy at least.

> The unit tests fail because WinKerberos doesn't define GSSError, a subclass of KrbError.

Huh. I completely missed that. Easy enough to add.

You're right about the fact that using HTTP without HTTPS is a bad idea, but people do that and may rely on Kerberos w/ mutual authentication to protect them as much as it's able.

Another problem is that enabling canonicalization by default could break things for people which are currently working. If they've configured their kerberos environment to not canonicalize names using DNS they're relying on their servers to have access to keys for the CNAME rather than the target of the CNAME.

Also, canonicalization can mean different things to different people. Some environments canonicalize by resolving CNAMEs (dns_canonicalize_hostname) , while others canonicalize by resolving CNAMEs and PTRs (rdns).

I don't think it's a good idea to change the default in a way that takes away an environment admins ability to configure their environment, even in the case of mutual authentication being disabled. 

... as a compromise, what about the possibility of making the defaults configurable within the library? Meaning, we make 2 module level constants, DEFAULT_DNS_CANONICALIZE_HOSTNAME and DEFAULT_RDNS, set them both to False by default, and add two constructor arguments on HTTPKerberosAuth named dns_canonicalize_hostname and rdns, and have them default to whatever the module level constant values are.

With them both off (the default), it will defer to whatever the sites sysadmins configured their kerberos libraries to do, but you can change the default once in the module so you don't need to do it independently for every instance created.

That is a good point. My concern is that our staunch adherence to the "only one approach" means more code for people in simple cases.

That is, if we stick to the object-oriented approach, one-liners aren't possible because of our decision to never allow chaining:

```python
# This is *NOT* possible:
topic = client.topic('name').create()
```

If we go with the imperative approach, one liners are possible, but it means the logic gets mixed up a bit:

```python
topic = client.create_topic()  # What does this return? What can you do with the result?
```

I'm suggesting we actively decide to make the object-oriented approach the authoritative one, with shortcut helper methods for common simple tasks where a common user would want a one-liner.

```python
class Client(...):
  def create_topic(self, *args, **kwargs):
    topic = self.topic(*args, **kwargs)
    topic.create()
    return topic
```

This way, all logic lives in one place, but we get the one-liners a lot of users want (me among them)

@hugovk: very few new downloads over the past month for EOL versions of python means that very few people are choosing to install `dill` on old resources -- it does not mean, however, that support for the EOL versions should be dropped.  The main driver I've used thus far in selecting which versions of python to support is to look at what versions of python are being supported on the national computing infrastructure (i.e. machines at LANL, ORNL, BNL, LLNL, etc).  A particular version of python ships with linux (or whatever is used, commonly RHEL), and that particular version is often what is supported on the machine until it is retired.  This definition typically corresponds fairly well to a linux distribution's EOL.  On these types of clusters (as well, to a lesser extent, in a corporate cluster environment), the user often doesn't have the opportunity to upgrade to a newer python.  Some do, but many don't.  It's my experience that most institutional clusters are running python 2.6-2.7, and/or python 3.4-3.6 -- however, I haven't done a careful study lately, and 2.6 may no longer be supported.  So, in my opinion, the definition of when to drop a version should be somewhere between python EOL and linux EOL.  Meaning, 3.3 and below are probably no longer necessary to support, and 2.6 is questionable (and probably can go, but I'll have to verify that).

解释一下:

你的 server 填写 "::", ssserver 监听的端口应该是 "::1:", google 并测试了一下

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13357301/35614069-cc31bd42-06a8-11e8-87f7-b0a0afb6dc83.png)

这个地址相当于 ipv4 中的 '127.0.0.1', 也就是说你的 ssserver 没有开放到公网上面, 这是一个私有地址, ssclient 无法连接上来.

当你改成 '0.0.0.0' 的时候, 会监听你的 VPS 的公网地址, 这时候你的 ssclient 可以连接 ssserver, 当你访问 ipv6.google.com 的时候, 你和 ssserver 的通讯依旧是 ipv4 协议的通讯, ssserver 收到 ipv6.google.com 请求的时候会请求解析该域名的IP地址, 用 `curl -v ipv6.google.com` 测试了一下:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13357301/35614209-42cfb4fe-06a9-11e8-9f44-e2ae982790cb.png)

解析出来地址 **2404:6800:4005:800::200e**, 国外的 vps 很多都是支持 ipv6 通讯的, 所以 vps 和 ipv6.google.com 服务器通讯, 然后拿到数据, 然后还是 ipv4 协议回传给 ssclient(数据已经被加密).

I don't have a strong opinion on this one either way.

But my general feeling is that the current documentation setup biases toward HEAD, and thus (correctly) sends a signal that people should bias toward using more recent release up to date. We're not investing a bunch of time in the very-long-term stability of older releases: without a lot-lot more folks consuming and contributing patches for older releases, they are not going to be "supported" for more than two or three months (ie two or three stable versions).

If there is another good way to send that signal (perhaps big red badges on older versions indicating that they are "unsupported") then maybe the messaging would still be accurate. But regardless, I think that if we're not careful here, we might implicitly make promises that we cannot keep... and so it's a low priority for me.

I had the same problem, and also wanted to set the `IAM Role` property for the stack. The `cloudformation deploy` command doesn't offer either of these as options.

We worked around it by creating our stacks as a pre-requisite steps using a non-SAM cloud formation template. It just contains a very basic template with a temporary lambda function and role.

```
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: 2010-09-09
Description: Dummy template for new stacks
Resources:
  role: 
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      RoleName: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-temporary-role"
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version : 2012-10-17
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service:
                - lambda.amazonaws.com
            Action: sts:AssumeRole
  lambda:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Function
    Properties:
      Code:
        ZipFile: "Dummy code"
      Handler: dummy
      Role: !GetAtt role.Arn
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
```

Then creating the stack using the following command:

`cloudformation create-stack --stack-name my-stack --template-url dummy-template.yaml --role-arn ... --tags ... --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM`

This allows you to supply role-arn and tags arguments. Then once created, you can then run your SAM deployment over the top.

> Hmm, this is interesting. I haven't come across annotation and documentation strings in the schema before. Where did you pull these from?

The schema files I use are in the code tree here (and in the directory just above):
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/blob/master/ref/xsd/wml.xsd

this is a really interesting document:

[xml-reference](https://www.ecma-international.org/news/TC45_current_work/Office%20Open%20XML%20Part%204%20-%20Markup%20Language%20Reference.pdf)

> The code reproduces the XML documentation example, however my implementation starts at 1. whereas word starts at zero. This should probably be in line.

I don't see a problem with starting at 1. In fact, I like it better. There's always the chance that `0` is a special case in some application and there's no reason at all to keep them in any order or to make sure we use up any values that we find unused. They're strictly an arbitrary unique identifier. Having a quick look at the schema though, it looks like there are many objects that share this "id" space. I'm inclined to keep this value unique across all `w:id` attributes throughout the document.

> Are bookmarks enforced unique?
> The word document allows for duplicate keys and it does not trigger a repair error if we there are.
> However, we could implement a warning when aduplicate key is added?
> 

Btw, there should be no way a user (developer using python-docx) can add a duplicate key. Remember it is the ID value that is the key, not the name. We will always be the one to choose the ID for a new bookmark. While a user may be able to discover what that ID is, we will not empower them to change it.

If there are already duplicates in the .docx file, we at least won't make things any worse.

- remove the paragraph

Then, the next table will have the deleted paragraph's font size.

Code:
```
doc.add_paragraph('')
paragraph = doc.paragraphs[-1]
paragraph.style.font.size = Pt(font_size)
p = paragraph._element
p.getparent().remove(p)
p._p = p._element = None
doc.add_table(...)
```

Your middle paragraph is how I intended the code to work:

> I thought if matchAmbiguous is True, identical matches are counted in identicalMatchCount, and ambiguous matches in the ambiguousMatchCount. If matchAmbiguous is False, identical matches are still counted, but the ambiguousMatchCount is always 0 (as in the block of code above).

But your final sentence (which is right):

> So then the only thing to fix would be that the identicalMatchCount shouldn't count identical ambiguous matches (which is what my changes do).

Doesn't correspond to your code, because in your description above you're talking about the case where `matchAmbiguous` is False (which is what #573 is also discussing), but your code change isn't done in that context. So with your code, if `matchAmbiguous` is passed as `False`, `nonGapMismatchCount` will be incremented, which is wrong.

Try passing `matchAmbiguous=False` in your test.  That's what this ticket is about and you're not passing that, so you're actually working on something else :-)

BTW, my original plan with this function was to classify all the matches/non-matches exactly and to pass back all the information to the caller. Then the caller can decide what to do. They might want to calculate a matching fraction, or just know how many gaps there were, etc. I started out wanting to just know the fraction of matching nucleotides, but that's too simplistic a question.

MultiGPU training generally works, but with some edge case failures so it's left undocumented and not officially supported.  Pass a list of devices, e.g. `cuda_device=[0, 1, 2, 3]` instead of a single integer to `Trainer`.  We only implement data parallelism now, and when training with multiple GPUs we split each batch across the GPUs then aggregate gradients before updating parameters.  As a result, when training with multiple GPUs set the batch_size in your configuration should be `num_gpus * batch_size_single_gpu`.

Also, to @carmstrong's question: we were running HVM and seeing this problem. Since then, there have been folks who have presented problems with PV as well (an 8 second drift on one node report in the past couple of days). When a problem is encountered with PV clock drift, my current thinking is that the underlying Xen host is having NTP issues, and PV guests inherit this clock drift through the hypervisor as a result.

Running ntp inside a VM with an independent wallclock is asking for drift, as jiffies get stolen by other bursty bad actors on the same Xen host.

Trusting the underlying Xen host will always have correct time with no drift seems to have been proven wrong at least occasionally as well. The two solutions here are:

1. To enable an independent wallclock on PV and run ntpd (which conflicts with the point above), which we know to be problematic with HVM today.

2. Shutdown/stop the PV instance and start it back up, so that the instance gets moved to another Xen host in the AWS farm that potentially has the correct NTP time.

3. Fiddle with `mon-clock-drift-allowed` with the guidance of the Ceph project and see if we can't allow a little more wiggle room than 0.05 seconds for ntp and stolen jiffies in a VM:

http://ceph.com/docs/master/rados/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-mon/#clock-skews

Okay, after looking through the [Unicode FAQ](http://www.unicode.org/faq/utf_bom.html#BOM) after being sent there by some Python core devs.  It appears out current behavior is incorrect, and we should do things as in this PR, because the `UTF-{16,32}{BE,LE}` codecs are only meant to be used when there is no BOM!  The FAQ says:

>  Use the tag UTF-16BE to indicate big-endian UTF-16 text, and UTF-16LE to indicate little-endian UTF-16 text. If you do use a BOM, tag the text as simply UTF-16.

Furthermore it goes on to say that:

> Where the data has an associated type, such as a field in a database, a BOM is unnecessary. In particular, if a text data stream is marked as UTF-16BE, UTF-16LE, UTF-32BE or UTF-32LE, a BOM is neither necessary nor *permitted*. Any U+FEFF would be interpreted as a ZWNBSP.

@sigmavirus24, This is truly an error on our part, as the old behavior was reporting the wrong codec.

I think your comment is saying that you think a whitelist can work, but just in case it isn't, here's my reasoning.

A whitelist should be:
  - future proof against new commands showing up
  - most reliable reduction in p2p cmd traffic over the queues
  - subscribers can be written to throw errors on unexpected commands making things more explicit

It should be easy for the `Peer` class to detect when it has no subscribers for a message type and to log a warning (like I think you were saying above).

Steps to fix your environment if you're currently broken:

1. Note the *location* of your current base conda environment.  For the example here, let's say it's at `C:\Users\Scooby\Anaconda2`.  You'll also need to know the python version you had before you encountered these errors.  Let's say for this example it's python 3.5.
2. Download miniconda from https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Windows-x86_64.exe
3. Run the miniconda installer, choose install for "Just Me", and then specify a Destination Folder that's *different* than your current base environment.  For this example, we'll put it at `C:\temp-conda`.
4. In any command prompt, run the command `C:\temp-conda\Scripts\conda.exe install -p C:\Users\Scooby\Anaconda2 python=3.5 conda`
5. Your normal conda should now be healed, and you can now remove the `C:\temp-conda` directory if you like.
6. When you ran the new miniconda installer, your "Anaconda Prompt" shortcut may have been changed.  If it no longer works correctly, right click on the "Anaconda Prompt" icon, choose "Properties", and make the the `Target` field points to `C:\Users\Scooby\Anaconda2` instead of `C:\temp-conda`.  Note that you might have to make two changes to the `Target` field instead of just one.  Since the field box is pretty small, it's usually easier to select the field, hit Ctrl-A then Ctrl-C to select all text the copy the text, paste the text into an empty notepad file, make your edits, and then copy and paste the edited text back in the `Target` field.

The wakari-compute installer is a cas-installer that creates an environment called wakari-compute (our app runs from this environment). It also creates a separate, anaconda environment for our users by including the Anaconda 2.3.0 linux-64 installer under extra_files and running it during the post-install step.

We need to install some additional user packages into the Anaconda environment we just created. In the 0.8.0 release, we include the package tarballs under extra_files, and then conda install them.

In 0.9.0, we took those packages and built them into a cas-installer, and we run that after the Anaconda installer. That's what I was referring to as a "secondary installer".

The main point is not to avoid installing VUnit (which is only 6MB, including the docs), but to avoid installing Python:

- `debian:stretch-slim`, which is the base I use, is 55.3 MB.
- `python:slim-stretch`: 141.5 MB.

So, installing python in each container requires four times as much space for the base image. In practice, this is the difference:

- `ghdl/ghdl:stretch-mcode` 189.3 MB.
- `ghdl/ext:vunit-master` 304.6 MB.

Moreover, if external, VUnit itself could be executed in a `python:3.6-alpine3.7` container, that requires 'only' 83.4 MB (a half of the Debian version). Ideally, GHDL would also be distributed based on some alpine image, but unfortunately, GHDL cannot be compiled for alpine.

A different point, which I don't know how relevant might be, is that `ghdl/ext` images are based on a single python version. So different images are required if other python versions are needed. For example, right now it is hard to integrate docker in the travis matrix of vunit.

play-with-docker is meant to be a playground for docker and kubernetes. Users are expected to create multiple nodes/containers/machines, connect them, orchestrate the services, etc. That's overkill as a HDL playground, where a single node suffices. However, it is really useful to have a machine with 4GB that lasts for 4h, where you can run "any" distro you want with any tool.

This issue is not about a playground for non-VUnit users, although it can be used as so. The greatest value from my point of view is that VUnit users can report issues, without having to upload the sources that reproduce the issue to any external place. Right now, I need to add them to a repo of my own, to a Gist, etc. Then, maintainers/collaboratos, need to understand how to get them in order to execute and reproduce the example. Sometimes it is straightforward, sometimes minor modifications are required, and sometimes the OP does not provide enough info to reproduce it. This let's the user ensure that the collaborator will get exactly the same results.

---

Anyway, I see that the `issue-runner` scripts can be used not only with VUnit, but with any project in GitHub. Therefore, I am going to split it to it's own repo.

---

I really like EDA Playground as a concept. Indeed, 1138-4EB/hwd-ide is a prototype of a similar solution. However, there are some important limitations right now:

- AFAIK, it is free as in 'free beer'. Not free software. Not even open source.
- *Each run is limited to 60 seconds runtime and 100MB of memory*.
- It is lacking GHDL and VUnit support.
- There is no straightforward mechanism to download the playground (the sources that compose a project) and execute it locally.

Should it be open, we could easily add all the other features and even more. Indeed, should EDA Playground be available as a docker image (including the subset of free tools: iverilog, yosys, GHDL, VUnit, etc.), it'd be straightforward for each user to run it's own EDA playground inside play-with-docker or in their own machines. Thus, overcoming the runtime and memory limitation, without Doules being required to add better servers.

Just as an example, this is a contribution I did some months ago to https://github.com/portainer/portainer There is an 'official' demo in a private server that is available at  http://demo.portainer.io, but it goes down quite frequently. So, I provided [PWD/?stack=portainer-demo/play-with-docker/docker-stack.yml](http://play-with-docker.com/?stack=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/portainer/portainer-demo/master/play-with-docker/docker-stack.yml) as an alternative. It reproduces the 'official' demo in a PWD instance.

Aha! That's ringing a bell now.  I'm guessing this would either have to be something on the Elasticsearch side (not likely), or you are connecting through a proxy or load balancer (or timed firewall, or some other kind of intermediary).  

Curator itself does not attempt retries with Snapshots.  The `Failed to complete` error you see there is just Curator reporting what it received back from the Elasticsearch API.  The precise timing here of 10 minutes, like the 60 seconds of the previous post, suggests there's an intermediary between Curator and the client node.  The timeout value may be 21600, but the intermediary (be it a load balancer, proxy, firewall, etc.) times out the connection at an exact value.

As stated, Curator itself has no control over this, and does not attempt a retry as part of the code (see the code in question [here](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.3.post1/curator/actions.py#L832-L847).  The urllib3 python module, which is used by the Elasticsearch python module that Curator depends on, will do exactly what is described here if an intermediary interrupts the connection.  It will retry/re-send the last request.  That would explain why the [code that tests whether a snapshot is already running](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v4.2.3.post1/curator/actions.py#L830-L831) is not catching anything.

If there is an intermediary of some kind, I can't solve that for you with code changes to Curator.  If you must use Curator, you _can_ set `wait_for_completion: False` and it will just return immediately, and the snapshot will run in the background.  No timeout will be encountered.  

It may be possible to write a special "wait_for_completion" action that will cover cases like this.  It will simply check that a snapshot, forceMerge, or whatever task is running, and keep checking every so many seconds until either a timeout is reached, or the action completes successfully.  Since 5.0 supports this kind of task architecture already, I may put that into Curator 5.  It will be a bit hacky to add that into Curator 4, but I might do it anyway.  Otherwise, your best bet is to either use `wait_for_completion: False` or get a connection to Elasticsearch that isn't behind a proxy or load balancer (or whatever the intermediary is), or set a ridiculously high timeout for the Curator box's connection to said intermediary.

    import csv
    import datetime
    import resource

    from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch


    class Timer(object):

        def __init__(self):
            self._start = None
            self._stop = None

        def start(self):
            self._start = datetime.datetime.now()

        def stop(self):
            self._stop = datetime.datetime.now()

        def delta(self):
            return self._stop - self._start


    class Counter(object):

        def __init__(self, initial=0):
            self._total = initial

        def increase(self, amount=1):
            self._total += amount

        def total(self):
            return self._total


    def report(counter=None, timer=None):
        if counter:
            print "total: %s" % counter.total()

        if timer:
            print "time: %s" % timer.delta().seconds


    class BaseRelevance(object):

        def __init__(self, in_name, out_name):
            self._in_name = in_name
            self._out_name = out_name

        def name(self, **kwargs):
            raise NotImplementedError

        def __call__(self):
            with open(self._in_name) as in_file,  \
                    open(self._out_name, "a") as out_file:

                reader = csv.reader(in_file)
                writer = csv.writer(out_file)

                counter = Counter()
                timer = Timer()
                timer.start()

                print "elasticsearch - memory usage %s" % resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF).ru_maxrss

                for row in reader:
                    identity, username, full_name, first_name, last_name, email = \
                        row
                    counter.increase()

                    name = self.name(
                        identity=identity,
                        username=username,
                        full_name=full_name.decode("utf-8"),
                        first_name=first_name.decode("utf-8"),
                        last_name=last_name.decode("utf-8"),
                        email=email,
                    )
                    query = {'query': {'filtered': {
                        'filter': {'bool': {'must': [
                            {'terms': {'person_types': (
                                'A',
                                'B',
                                'C',
                                'D')}}]}},
                        'query': {'bool': {'must': [
                            {'bool': {'should': [
                                {'match': {'name': {
                                    'fuzziness': 2,
                                    'operator': 'and',
                                    'query': name}}},
                                {'match': {'last_name': {
                                    'fuzziness': 2,
                                    'query': name}}}]}}]}}}}}
                    es = Elasticsearch([{"host": "localhost", "port": 9200}])
                    results = es.search(index="myindex", body=query, size=100)
                    results = results["hits"]["hits"]

                    found = len(results)
                    rank = 0
                    for result in results:
                        result = result["_source"]
                        rank += 1

                        if result["identity"] == identity:
                            writer.writerow([
                                identity,
                                full_name,
                                name.encode("utf-8"),
                                found,
                                rank,
                            ])
                            break
                    else:
                        writer.writerow([
                            identity,
                            full_name,
                            name.encode("utf-8"),
                            found,
                            0,
                        ])

                    if not (counter.total() % 100):
                        print "elasticsearch - processed %s" % counter.total()
                        print "elasticsearch - memory usage %s" % resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF).ru_maxrss

                timer.stop()
                report(counter=counter, timer=timer)


    class RelevanceFull(BaseRelevance):

        def name(self, full_name, **kwargs):
            return full_name

    relevance_full = RelevanceFull("names.csv", "relevance_full.csv")


    class RelevanceLast(BaseRelevance):

        def name(self, last_name, **kwargs):
            return last_name

    relevance_last = RelevanceLast("names.csv", "relevance_last.csv")


    class RelevanceLastFirst(BaseRelevance):

        def name(self, last_name, first_name, **kwargs):
            return last_name + " " + first_name

    relevance_last_first = RelevanceLastFirst("names.csv", "relevance_last_first.csv")

Two concerns:

The --admin-enabled argument would push the parameter into ACR and persist the flag of the service to allow/disallow admin operations. Would it be desirable to allow user to omit the parameters? I would think this would confuse the user and lead them to think --admin-enabled is just a simple modifier of the operation. Moreover, “--admin-enabled” actually has 3 valid values, “true/false” meaning change to “ture/false”; not having the flag means keep the current value. Making this flag a switch and allow user to provide no input to my view would confuse the user and have them think it’s a boolean switch.

After reading the code, it seems like if we want to go ahead and implement this, it would be a pretty big change. The framework automatically use (extract_args_from_signature)[https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/_introspection.py#L62] to scan the method signature and determine what type of parameter is needed from command line. (required/optional, default value, etc) It looks like there's no simple way of doing what is asked for here. @tjprescott, please correct me if I am wrong.

You can always do something like this:

```py
  sock = socket.socket()
  sock.bind(("127.0.0.1", 0))
  # Aiohttp will call 'listen' inside.
  # But it must be called before we actually use the port,
  # any attempts to connect before the 'listen' call will
  # be rejected.
  sock.listen(128)
  _, port = sock.getsockname()
  print("Port: %d" % (port,))

  aiohttp.web.run_app(app, sock=sock)
```

I use similar code in unittests. Note that while 'listen' is optional and kinda ugly, there is no way to reliably 'wait' for listen call by aiohttp (as on_startup is called before setting up asyncio servers). As about this weird looking '128' param - took it from aiohttp defaults.

Hope that helps )

In what sort of deployment? The KernelManager selects the ports, so you can use a custom KernelManager to select ports in a different way. You would just set the various port attributes on the KM:

```python
p = 5000
km.hb_port = p+1
km.shell_port = p+2
km.control_port = p+3
km.stdin_port = p+4
km.iopub_port = p+5
```

You cannot generally use a config file to specify kernel ports, since it would mean that all kernels would try to use the same ports and collide. That is, unless you are starting one kernel per IP. You can tell it to use ipc instead of tcp with:


```python
c.KernelManager.transport = 'ipc'
```

This was changed in #3356 and refined in #3593. The idea is that when it's asked to listen on all interfaces, it shows the hostname of the computer it's running on, which can work from another computer.

Your docker command maps the container port to a port on localhost, but the notebook running in the container doesn't know about that. E.g. you could ask Docker to put it on a different port, like 8889, and it would print out the URL with `8888`, because that's what the notebook server knows about. So it's kind of a coincidence that the `localhost:8888` URL works, albeit a fairly common coincidence.

That looks like it's an error moving a file to trash, which we do with the package [send2trash](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/send2trash).

The implementation is trying to find out:

1. Is this file on the same device as $HOME?
2. If not, what is the mount point of the device it's on?

We've seen some cases where these checks can go wrong when inside a container, but that looked a bit different. We added our own check to only trash files that are on the same device as home - this may be a good point to start debugging:

My notes. Please add anything I left out. TODO new peru features in bold.

- People have asked for tags / sync profiles / filters / something, to handle things like dev-only dependencies. There are a lot of different ways this could work. Probably the easiest thing will be to think about how we would want `peru.yaml` to look in this world (or whether it might split into more than one file), and work backwards from there.

> Using locals() is kind of like import *

The approach that I used for `import *` was to create a special class of binding `StarImportation`, which allowed for a different error to be reported, which could then be ignored if desired.

The same could be done with `locals()` , to mark all names as used by the `locals()` , but that usage would be replaced by any other more specific usage of the name.

Then the report would show which names in the scope are *only* used by `locals()` , and that more specific error could then be ignored when appropriate.

https://github.com/PyCQA/pyflakes/pull/343 fixes this bug, and provides the basis for reporting unused variables differently if they were only used by `locals()`

Yeah, I guess. I got to the bottom of it and came up with the "right" workaround to use in my code. This is still technically an issue, but it has nothing to do with `xdist` as it turned out - it was only exposed because `execnet` has a special case for replacing `sys.stderr` on Windows.

The core issue is that `conftest.py` *may or may not* be executed with output capturing enabled, depending on whether the args passed to `pytest` indicate that the particular `conftest.py` is the *initial* conftest or not. This, combined with the `logging` module storing a reference to `sys.stderr`, *and* `execnet` replacing `sys.stderr` with a file whose fileno is not 2, causes logging output to not be captured.

Unfortunately, it is required by the [ACME spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-01#section-7.2) that the initial request goes over port 80.

With that said, there are a couple workarounds for this issue. One solution is to setup a redirect from port 80 to port 443. This will enable you to complete the challenges.

Another solution is to perform `tls-sni-01` challenges instead of `http-01` challenges. The former goes over port 443. To use this approach, however, you must either:
1. Be running Apache and use the `apache` plugin.
2. Use the `standalone` plugin which needs to bind to port 443, meaning you'd have to stop your webserver.

Finally, while it won't be implemented for a few months, you may be able to use [DNS challenges](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-01#section-7.5) to perform domain validation in the future.

I understand thath these solutions have drawbacks, but I believe they are your best options given the current state of the ACME spec. Since this repo is for the Let's Encrypt client which is bound by the ACME spec, I'm marking this as `wontfix`. I hope my suggestions help.

Most of my work will be in letsencrypt-auto. Rough plan:

* We'll put a peep-armored reqs.txt in the tree somewhere, along with a signature of its SHA256.
* letsencrypt-auto will go figure out what the latest version of LE is (from PyPI or GH--over HTTPS to make it harder to artificially keep LE out of date) then wget that version's reqs.txt and signature off GH.
* It'll verify the sig with `openssl` (already in the bootstrap scripts and easy to airgap sigs for).
* Then it'll peep install that reqs.txt into the venv.
* Optionally, it can update letsencrypt-auto itself by somehow getting it (make a Python package to install it into venv/bin, for example) and then copying it over the current version. The file descriptor of the current version should stay valid through the end of the shell script, so we should be able to get away with that.

Pros:

* We don't need GPG (which is hard to airgap sigs for) or git.
* We install everything from our PyPI release artifacts rather than having everybody rebuild from source checkouts. This would matter more in a world where PyPI hosted Linux binary wheels and we could save the complexity of C compilation on client machines.

Accepted risks:

* We dynamically produce a requirements.txt from the latest constraint-satisfying versions of packages in release.sh. We'll verify author signatures where they exist and leave the rest up to the fates. At least it'll be repeatable. MITMs of the release machine should result in a flood of "Hey, my hashes didn't match" tickets.

The assign() method actually allows you to set the initial offset for each partition, so try something like this:

```
    def on_assign (c, ps):
        for p in ps:
            p.offset=-2
        c.assign(ps)

    c.subscribe(['test'], on_assign=on_assign)

```

There are currently no symbolic names for the special logical offsets (stored, invalid, beginning, end,..), so you'll need to use internal values for them:  -2 for beginning and -1 for end. In the above example I set it to -2 to always start consuming from the earliest/beginning offset, but you can also set it to an absolute offset (e.g., 12352) if you are storing the offsets outside Kafka.

1. I'm not a regular Singularity user, is there a particular advantage to using a Singularity image over converting a Docker image?

This remains a false dichotomy and a misstatement of my position. The proposed solution works fine in 95% of cases - it should surprise no one to learn that Homebrew, Conda, MSI's enviornment modules, [Debian](https://packages.debian.org/wheezy/bwa), and [Nix](http://git.savannah.gnu.org/cgit/guix.git/tree/gnu/packages/bioinformatics.scm#n775) all call bwa... bwa. Small variations will of course pop up - but those can be solved with site or package-manager specific mappings on the backend - small tweaks - there is no need for long lists of IDs in tools. If you are using environment modules - you may need to map snap to snap-aligner locally - for Debian we can even build community mappings.

I'm arguing for one ID per component not long lists. If you feel it needs a namespace fine - if you feel some service needs to do the mapping - I'm fine with that too - go work with bio.tools on that and this can leverage that work.  But it should be a goodie, an add-on, a best practice - I don't think CWL tools should require registration of their components in biotools to work - it should just work. This just works.

You are imaging a potential system for these things - Galaxy is a real and existing system that supports multiple package managers today and this problem hasn't really materialized - and we have concrete ideas for how to address it in a lightweight way.

I am pretty sure now that the translational frameshift mentioned in my previous comment is the reason why `NM_002537.3:c.250del` results in a `p.?` variant. I checked several transcripts in the results list from the query in the initial post and all of them have a translational frameshift, and mapping results in `p.?`.

Using ensembl data the mapping worked for me, since they solve the issue by the introduction of "frameshift introns", see, e.g., [ENST00000326005](grch37.ensembl.org/Homo_sapiens/Transcript/Summary?t=ENST00000326005) which corresponds to [NM_002537](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/669250888).

As far as I can see, no information about translational frameshifts can be found in UTA, so there perhaps is no way to fix this issue in the code without changes to the database.

In principle, the two bugs regarding the padding and the translational frameshifts are unrelated, but they always occur together when using UTA data, because the length of the coding sequence only differs from multiples of 3 if a translational frameshift occurs (although there might be other rare cases where it is not related to a frameshift, but I could not find an example yet).

Here's my code to make IV curves. I'm still not sure if any of this should be included in pvlib or not. I think we should at least add something similar to this to the documentation. Maybe someone else has a better way to do this.

```python

def fivepoints_to_frame(pnt):
    """
    Converts a 1 dimensional, dict-like singlediode or sapm result
    to a 5 row DataFrame with columns current and voltage.
    Users can iterate over the rows of a multidimensional
    singlediode or sapm result, if necessary.
    """
    ivframe = {'i_sc': (pnt['i_sc'], 0),
               'p_mp': (pnt['i_mp'], pnt['v_mp']),
               'i_x': (pnt['i_x'], 0.5*pnt['v_oc']),
               'i_xx': (pnt['i_xx'], 0.5*(pnt['v_oc']+pnt['v_mp'])),
               'v_oc': (0, pnt['v_oc'])}
    ivframe = pd.DataFrame(ivframe, index=['current', 'voltage']).T
    ivframe = ivframe.sort_values(by='voltage')
    
    return ivframe

resistance_shunt = 16
resistance_series = 0.094
nNsVth = 0.473
saturation_current = 1.943e-09
photocurrent = 7
module_parameters = pvsystem.retrieve_sam('cecmod')['Example_Module']

v_oc = pvsystem.v_from_i(resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, 0, saturation_current, photocurrent)
voltage = np.linspace(0, v_oc, 100)

current = pvsystem.i_from_v(resistance_shunt, resistance_series, nNsVth, voltage, saturation_current, photocurrent)

fivepnts = pvsystem.singlediode(
    module_parameters, photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)
ivframe = fivepoints_to_frame(fivepnts)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(voltage, current)
ax.scatter(ivframe['voltage'], ivframe['current'], c='k', s=36, zorder=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, None)
ax.set_ylim(0, None)
ax.set_ylabel('current (A)')
ax.set_xlabel('voltage (V)')
```

I just checked out the _ruamel_ package and it indeed supports loading/dumping YAML comments. Here is a small example of how to load a YAML configuration and access its comments:
```
from ruamel.yaml import YAML

# could also be loaded from a file with `open("config.yaml")`
yaml_string = """\
# example
name:
    # details
    family: Smith   # very common
    given: Alice    # one of the siblings
"""

yaml = YAML()
code = yaml.load(yaml_string)
code['name']['given'] = 'Bob'

print(code.ca)
print(code.ca.items['name'][3][0].value)
print(code["name"].ca)

```
Output:
```
Comment(comment=[None, [CommentToken('# example\n', line: 0)]],
  items={'name': [None, None, None, [CommentToken('# details\n', line: 2)]]})
# details
Comment(comment=[None, [CommentToken('# details\n', line: 2)]],
  items={'family': [None, None, CommentToken('# very common\n', line: 3), None], 'given': [None, None, CommentToken('# one of the siblings\n', line: 4), None]})
```
The regular YAML entries are accessed as nested directories and the corresponding comments have to be retrieved with the `ca` attribute. Each `CommentToken` can be in either of 4 different places depending on the position of the comment in the YAML file.

More details on how to use the comments can be found [here](https://bitbucket.org/ruamel/yaml/src/1507ba09ded5e10d16f6555254d8b63cb3fd2b8b/comments.py?fileviewer=file-view-default). Unfortunately the general documentation is not very thorough.

@rainoftime The "bit-blasting bit-vector solver" is not a single, isolated piece of code. It is implemented via multiple preprocessing tactics (i.e. you need the complete tactic framework), then the SAT solver, but also the SMT solver, in case there are uninterpreted functions in the problem (e.g. bit-vector division by 0). So, in the end you'll need pretty much everything anyways... I'm not sure this will be worth the effort. 

In the past I've also compiled Z3 to a static library ( `--staticlib` on `mk_make.py` ). This may help to get the size of your binaries down a little, if the compiler finds that some things aren't used. But, I think the main reason for the huge binary is the extensive use of C++ templates and inlined functions, resulting in lots of duplicated binary code. It would be great if someone could look into that! Are there any tools that try to identify such problems?

To help the developers track this down it would help if you provided a backtrace (for all threads) when the crash occurs. You can do this by running `z3` under a debugger.

For example

```
gdb --args /path/to/z3 your_file.smt2
```

If you're using gdb as your debugger see [this](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/18391808/getting-the-backtrace-for-all-the-threads-in-gdb). If you're using lldb as your debugger see [this](https://lldb.llvm.org/lldb-gdb.html) (I think the command is `bt all`). Note with gdb there is [convenient functionality to log all output to a file](https://sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/Logging-Output.html).

If the bug is not obvious you may wish to compile Z3 with AddressSanitizer to look for memory access related bugs.

Also, I have the following questions / suggestions after heavily reviewing the code:

* `fs.permissions`: `Permissions` should probably implement `__int__` 
* `fs.base`: `setbinfile`, `setfile` and `getfile` are really confusing: `getfile` and `setbinfile` require a binary file, `setfile` a text file, but has not `getfile` equivalent. I believe `getfile` should be renamed to `getbinfile`, and a text-file accepting `getfile` implemented (I know, this is backward incompatible).
* `fs.move`: `move_dir` could be implemented using a walker instead of `copy_dir`, to remove each file after it is moved instead of moving everything. This would be less storage-hungry, and easier to interrupt / resume transfers.
* `fs.multifs`: instead of using a namedtuple, we could implement a proper `_PrioritizedFS` class, and make it comparable. Why would we do that ?... so that we could use the `heapq` module and a priority queue instead of sorting the delegate filesystems over and over.

@rmatev I would be weary of calling DIRAC for the accessURL for _all_ LFN in a large list this can get stuck when a single file lookup takes a long time or the call can timeout if a user is asking for too many files at once.
I tried to work around problems like this in the splitter by having large calls broken up into blocks of 500 LFN at once which turned out to be less prone to communication problems. 

That being said I think `catalog.xml` is still generated by DIRAC and dropped on the WN as in principle a file can change it's PFN between the time of submission and the job running on the WN. It's only non-DIRAC backends that Ganga will manually generate this file as required which is the best solution to accessing LFN on a non-DIRAC system.

Certainly reducing the number of DIRAC calls does have performance benefits so good spot on this one.

If the job is being submitted locally (or to a batch system).
I would naively expect that we can do away with adding them into the sandbox and simply inject a few lines of code into the script which is run on the WN to extract/copy the files there.
(Or copy them directly to the WN from a request on the WN)

Wouldn't it make more sense to copy all shared files to the WN if the WN has access to the files and then auto-extract any (supported) archives in the WN script? (`__jobscript__` on Localhost).
If we pass the path of the shared folder to the job directly then we can use some generic copy/extract command which can be shared between the various backends.

It may be a good idea to add a configurable option to the backend to preserve this old functionality if it's required for a backend such as condor or lcg (I'm not an expert on either of these).

This is now a _problem_ with GaudiRun as the contents of the shared directory is now potentially very large. Other applications make use of this but it's GaudiRun that is taxing the way this works.

Hey @dingld,

1. I'm not familiar with deltafetch middleware, but from the code it looks like it can use 'deltafetch_key' meta key instead of request fingerprint (see https://github.com/scrapy-plugins/scrapy-deltafetch/blob/c13554474be4631c3e1d6e2e86c2b0ced86b817a/scrapy_deltafetch/middleware.py#L92). This should fix the issue. You can set it e.g. to request_fingerprint(request):

    ```
    request = SplashRequest(url, ...)
    request.meta['deltafetch_key'] = request_fingerprint(request)
    ``` 
 
2. Request from response can be just a scrapy.http.Request because of https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/issues/1890. Are you using disk queues? Even if request class is changed, why is this a problem in your case?

3. Real HTTP request is sent to Splash; it is a POST request to 127.0.0.1:8050. "Original" request is not sent anywhere; it is converted by scrapy-splash in order to render it whrough Splash instead of just downloading.

Hello,

The accuracy of the D400 series cameras varies slightly with distance but the error is <1% of distance. For example, if the object is 500mm away, you can expect accuracy <5mm. Of course this all depends on environmental factors such as lighting, material, etc. You can use the [Depth Quality Tool](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/development/tools/depth-quality) to measure depth accuracy.

I hope this helps.

Regards,
Jesus G.

Reminder: the root of the issue is that when you use the scratch_dir functionality in monty, the final contents of your original directory aren't the same as the final contents of the scratch_dir. Instead, the final contents of your original directory are the union of files from both - which is especially problematic if the last step in your scratch_dir operation is to gzip things whereas things started off not gzipped.

@Eraldo @hballard Yes, I think I should post an update here as I'm working full on subscriptions now.

Some thoughts about my journey: the way Apollo-Subscriptions use to manage subscriptions was not very friendly for the developer, needing to [hack around the resolution and a specific PubSub implementation](https://github.com/apollographql/graphql-subscriptions/blob/master/src/subscriptions-manager.ts#L169) that was "bypassing" the GraphQL engine for adapting it into subscriptions.
The reason for that is the GraphQL-js engine was not ready for subscriptions (meaning that was only able to return either a promise or a static value, but not a async iterator).

However GraphQL-js [recently added a way to subscribing](https://github.com/graphql/graphql-js/blob/master/src/subscription/subscribe.js) to a GraphQL query (that return an async iterator a.k.a. `Observable`) that let do simpler and cleaner implementations of subscriptions that decouple the subscription resolution from the "listener" on the subscription.

That led to better implementations of the transport mechanisms in GraphQL subscriptions like [subscriptions-transport-ws](https://github.com/apollographql/subscriptions-transport-ws).

So, in summary, subscriptions is something that should be bundled fully into the GraphQL engine, in a way that is easy to plug any mechanisms, such as:
* websockets with asyncio
* websockets with gevent
* websockets with django-daphne

And don't require any specific pub/sub mechanism and let this decision to the developer.


For the next version of Graphene, `2.0` I plan to have subscriptions bundled into the Engine :)
There is already a [branch in graphql-core](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/tree/features/subscriptions) where I'm doing the research process.

I will keep updating this thread with more information as I keep working on it.

I think I would expand this to “better tooling for examining the ast from resolver functions”

There has been a bit of discussion (graphql-python/graphene#348) about how get a list of selected fields in a resolver function.  The solutions proposed there work for only a subset of cases.  For example, `FragmentSpread` is handled, but `InlineFragment` is not.

What bothers me about the whole approach is that the executor already can do this, so it seems a pity to have to reinvent the wheel.  [`executor.resolve_field` gets `exe_context`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/2.0/graphql/execution/executor.py#L139), which I believe is an `ExecutionContext` instance, [which has a `get_sub_fields()` method](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/2.0/graphql/execution/base.py#L88), but does not pass `exe_context` to the resolver. @chaffeqa thanks but I've looked through the source code and it turns out graphene-django doesn't have any notion of `GenericRelation` or `GenericForeignKey` and can't process them. I had a look into adding the functionality myself and while I think it might be possible to get GenericRelation working (i.e. the 'parent' object's list of the generic children), because (as far as I can tell) graphene only supports the determination of node types 'statically' - without an instance of the object - it's impossible to generate a schema dynamically, i.e. one where the node type can only be determined using an actual instance (as in the case of Generic Relations).

I think the problem will need to be solved for Graphene - not being able to have generic relations is too limiting - but I think it will require changes to at least 2 of, if not all 3 of graphene-django, graphene and graphql-core.

Ok, we've been working on this out-of-band for a while and I wanted to summarize what we've found.

1. The problems with the userdata (custom/install script) occur when the execution context results in the usage of a particular TEMP path on Windows, `$env:windir\system32\config\systemprofile\AppData\Local\Temp`
2. We've found two issues around this TEMP path:
    * pip by default extracts the packages into the TEMP path, which results in MAXPATH errors for the bundled pshelp salt formula
    * apply_lgpo_delta writes and reads from the TEMP path and for some currently unknown reason this path causes apply_lgpo_delta to hang (as these particular files should not run into MAXPATH limits)
3. The problems are exposed when running on Azure with a Custom Script Extension because it seems the script is executed in the SYSTEM user context
    * TEMP starts out as `C:\temp`, but something in the python exe installer causes TEMP to be reset to the SYSTEM user's context `C:\system32\config\systemprofile\AppData\Local\Temp`
4. The problems **do not** occur in EC2 because the script is executed in the Administrator user's context, where TEMP is `C:\Users\Administrator\AppData\Local\Temp\`

We have a couple design goals in this area of the project, around the custom script, to keep in mind when coming up with a solution:

1. Keep the example custom script as simple and minimal as possible
2. Attempt to keep the custom script as similar as possible across platforms, windows and linux

Since windows requires more work to get a python application installed and running, we provide the watchmaker-bootstrap.ps1 script to  to get a windows system to the point where installing and using watchmaker is basically the same on windows as it is on linux. In support of that, we currently use the bootstrap to (also not ruling out other uses to address additional issues):

1. Install python (required for watchmaker)
2. Install git for windows (in a development environment)
3. Install certificates into the system cert store (in environments with custom CAs)
4. Manage necessary environment variables (such as refresh the PATH based on registry updates made by the installers)

As for potential solutions/workarounds for the issue at hand:

1. Use `Start-Process` to execute the bootstrap that installs python, and set the PATH in the custom script. This is the approach currently demonstrated [in this PR](https://github.com/plus3it/watchmaker/pull/491/files#diff-4be2078a125fe364f7198675d51bc688R137). This works because the custom script and the bootstrap script then run in different processes with different envs. So when the python installer in the bootstrap modifies the TEMP path, it doesn't not carry over to the parent custom script process.
2. (Needs testing) Update watchmaker-bootstrap.ps1 to manage the TEMP env. This approach would test for when the TEMP env is set to the SYSTEM user path and change it to the windir path. Something like:

    ```
    if ("$Env:TEMP" -eq "${Env:windir}\System32\config\systemprofile\AppData\Local\Temp")
    {
        $Env:TEMP, $Env:TMP = "${Env:windir}\Temp", "${Env:windir}\Temp"
    }
    ```

As it better supports the design goals, I would prefer option 2, if it does indeed work as I expect.

For now I'll only paste my solution to **Problem 2** mentioned above:

In settings: `AXES_USERNAME_FORM_FIELD = 'login'`

In `my_app.forms.py`:
```
from allauth.account.forms import LoginForm

class AllauthCompatLoginForm(LoginForm):
    def user_credentials(self):
        """
        For `axes` and `allauth` compatibility we simply add "login" key to the creentials
        dict so it is consistent with POST's "login" data key. 
        """
        credentials = super(AllauthCompatLoginForm, self).user_credentials()
        credentials['login'] = credentials.get('email') or credentials.get('username')
        return credentials
```

And then use this custom form in `urls.py`:
```
from allauth.account import views as allauth_views
from my_app.forms import AllauthCompatLoginForm

urlpatterns = [
    ...
    url(r'^accounts/login/$', # Override allauth's default view with our compatibility mod
        allauth_views.LoginView.as_view(form_class=AllauthCompatLoginForm),
        name="account_login"),
    url(r'^accounts/', include('allauth.urls')),
]
```

Also, **Problem 1** might be solved in more elegant way by wrapping the view in `urls.py` as you mentioned in issue #287

Hi,

you are right that the calling the apply function on the module extends the
graph. This is why one should only call apply function only during graph
building.

In your example sentence_batch = embedder(sentences) is outside of the
graph.

You can reorganize your code to be consistent with
https://groups.google.com/a/tensorflow.org/forum/#!msg/hub/ONjFEdpJFp8/si6Vpk_QAwAJ
:

g = tf.Graph()
with g.as_default():
input_sentences = tf.placeholder(tf.string, shape=[None])
embedder = tf_hub.Module("
https://tfhub.dev/google/universal-sentence-encoder/1")
sentence_batch = embedder(input_sentences)
table_op = tf.tables_initializer()
var_op = tf.global_variables_initializer()
g.finalize()  # You can already finalize here, not through session.graph.
It does not make a real difference, but is cleaner.

with tf.Session(graph=g) as session:
session.run([var_op, table_op])

for sentences in X:
  embeddings = session.run(sentence_batch, feed_dict={input_sentences:
sentences})


This doesn't prove that there isn't a memory leak on GPU, just shows that
your example can be reorganized to not bloat the graph.

There is no real fix for this other than manually deleting the stack. I think the underlying concept is that AWS wants you to be able to see the error which caused the stack creation to fail. Thats why a stack creation is rolled back but the stack itself stays in place.

There is a workaround available. You can configure the behaviour of this in your stack configuration:
```
on_failure: 'DO_NOTHING'|'ROLLBACK'|'DELETE'
```

How this looks like in your stack config:
```
region: eu-west-1
on_failure: ROLLBACK #configures for all stacks in this config file
stacks:
  my-stack:
    on_failure: DELETE #configures explicitly for my-stack
    ...
```

I'm not @SylvainCorlay, but this is a general problem with conda. It tries so hard to make installed packages relocatable that anything linked against libraries don't work unless they are installed in the conda root or fixed up by the conda-build process.

I work around this by using install_name_tool to fixup the library linkage during development. Here is a cmake macro that can fix a target.
```
#############################################################################################
# Fixup conda linking, if this python appears to be a conda python
get_filename_component(_python_bin_dir ${PYTHON_EXECUTABLE} DIRECTORY)
if (EXISTS "${_python_bin_dir}/conda")
    message("-- Detected conda python, activating workaround")
    set(_using_conda On)
else()
    set(_using_conda Off)
endif()

macro(fix_conda_python target)
if (_using_conda)
get_filename_component(_python_lib_file ${PYTHON_LIBRARY} NAME)
add_custom_command(TARGET ${target} POST_BUILD
                          COMMAND install_name_tool ARGS -change ${_python_lib_file} ${PYTHON_LIBRARY} $<TARGET_FILE:${target}>)
endif ()
endmacro()
```

Hi Ivan,

1. I agree that the ``forcecast`` option is problematic as a default. I've needed it in my own projects in the past when the conversion code was a bit too conservative and wouldn't accept some input (I don't have the full context, but it might have been something as innocent as converting a list of integers to complex floats). I would be happier with keeping it as a non-default option for ``array_t`` only.

2. All agreed about NPY_ARRAY_DEFAULT , NPY_ARRAY_IN_ARRAY, and NPY_ARRAY_OUT_ARRAY. These could map to enum values like ``default``, ``in_array`` and ``out_array``. It might be simpler to specify those as a template parameter to ``array_t`` rather than adding static methods.

3. Using ``PyArray_FromArray`` from the constructor when possible sounds reasonable, but it's important that non-arrays are handled as well (e.g. a standard python list). I would assume that ``PyArray_FromAny`` defers to ``PyArray_FromArray`` when given an array input, so the advantage is not 100% clear to me here.

4.  +1 for the ``astype`` proposal, that sounds very useful.

5. I think having a ``ensurecopy`` flag that can be disabled would be extremely useful (in conjunction with specifying an owner). This way, it would be possible to have dynamic C++ data structure that can return a NumPy view of themselves. On the user's side, this entails adding a binding method which creates a dtype on the fly and then returns a NumPy array with ``owner==this`` and ``ensurecopy`` not specified.

Seems like CouchDB is serving all the requests, including the ones to /.well-known/acme-challenge/... So certbot is unable to get the challenge files served. 

Your options boil down to:
1) Get CouchDB to serve requests to /.well-known/acme-challenge/ from filesystem, and use webroot plugin in certbot
2) Shut down CouchDB before certbot run, and use standalone plugin to answer the challenge. Restart CouchDB after
3) Set up a HTTP proxy in front of CouchDB, that would pick up requests to /.well-known/acme-challenge/ and serve them from filesystem, and proxy the rest to CouchDB. Use webroot plugin

I modified `__init__` to let us have our cake and eat it too. Instead of defaulting to `None`, continue to default to mock's sentinel because in this pattern we need to detect if the argument was changed or if the caller passed `None` as the argument. If the caller passed a return value deliberately, then `return_value` is added to the `_frozen_set` and set with the supplied value, otherwise it remains undefined and out of the set.

Another dirty sanity check to help you see what I'm thinking.
`
from certbot.tests.util import FreezableMock

return_value_set = FreezableMock(frozen=True, return_value=1)
print return_value_set._frozen_set # set(['_func', 'return_value', '_mock'])
try: # Expect this to fail
    return_value_set.return_value = 2
except AttributeError:
    print "Got expected attributeError during: %s" %("return_value_set.return_value = 2")
else:
    raise Exception("Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("return_value_set.return_value = 2"))

return_value_unset = FreezableMock(frozen=True)
print return_value_unset._frozen_set # set(['_func', '_mock'])
print return_value_unset() # mock obj

try: # Expect this to succeed
    return_value_unset.return_value = 2
except AttributeError:
    raise Exception("Should not get an AttributeError during: %s" %("return_value_unset.return_value = 2"))
else:
    print "Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("return_value_unset.return_value = 2")
try: # Expect this to succeed
    return_value_unset.return_value = 3
except AttributeError:
    raise Exception("Should not get an AttributeError during: %s" %("return_value_unset.return_value = 3"))
else:
    print "Succeeded setting return value on %s" %("return_value_unset.return_value = 3")

print return_value_unset() # 3

This is fixed by the balance proof's sequential nonce and the requirement of the two signatures. *Expose the API of raiden to external clients.*


## General API Spec:

- **Setting up** the initial connection to the raiden network should be possible **manually** (by opening channels and depositing into them from single API methods)
- Additionally we want to provide a **(semi-)automatic setup** to the network from given configuration-parameters 
*(e.g. based on available assets, so that the node takes care of an optimal connectivity to the raiden network by opening and depositing into channels without any additional considerations from the user)*
- API calls and responses should include a specification of the **API-version** (up from v1.0), so that the API is **future-proof**. The app has to include all versions of the API in the future.

- **MvP**: this version of the API needs to provide all neccessary tools to make it possible to build a **Wallet style UI** (issue #285)

## Protocol:

- Endpoints of all methods in the `raiden_service.RaidenAPI` are accessible through http-requests and are structured complying to a **RESTful**-pattern. Resources are kept minimal:
     - (e.g. on `/api/channel`
     -`GET` - get channel info,
     -`POST` - open channel,
     -`DELETE` - close channel,
      -`UPDATE` - deposit to channel )
- All http-requests (*-> client-initiated*) are *'blocking'* and at least include information about the **success of the call** in their response message. That way we don't have to work with callback-ids on the client side (see #280 ) but rather can work with promises.
- For **non-client initiated** actions (e.g. incoming transfers, opened channels, channel closing etc.) there will be the **emission of events** from the node to the client
- **Filtering** of the events will be done **on the client side**, so all events will be put in a FIFO-queue and sequentially forwarded to the client
- We assume that **only 1 client per node** is connected
- Events and responses will be **serialized to JSON**, 'difficult' data encoding will be reused from the Ethereum protocol (pyethapp/pyethereum) where possible
    (e.g. hex encoding for addresses)


## Transport:

- Method-calls: **TCP** (http-requests)
- Events:   the client can choose between
    1) TCP (**polling** http-requests)
    2) TCP ( **long-polling**; *'fake' push notifications*)

- The **interface** for the API has to be **uniform**, independent of the chosen transport-layer


## Further outlook:
### This is not part of this MvP!

- Calls and true push-events over **web-socket** connection(s)
- **Filtering** inside the node:
    - step 1) the client registers filters with a method call
    - step 2) either the client polls for filter changes
                    or the client listens for events pushed by the node

The basic idea is that `--queue queue` or `on_host='host'` would point to a configuration of `queue` or `host` that defines some common behaviors. Our job is to define these behaviors and implement them for particular types of `host`. The core would just grasp the `type` information and interact to them with a common interface. Something like:

```
remote-host:
    type: remote-server
   wait: True
   path-map:
   ...
```

```
rq1:
    type: redis-queue
    host:  redist-server-ip
    port: redist-server-port
    path-map:  if worker in the different file system
```

Then, we have classes defined for each type and registered as `entry points`. In the core, we

1. get the configuration of host.
2. get the type of host.
3. retrieve from entry point the class of host
4. create an object of that type of host and pass it the configuration
5. use this object to submit job and wait for result.

In this way, the system is very flexible in the defining of new queuing systems and for users

1. They can define a number of 'hosts' of different types, which can be local host, remote host, redis, or cluster.
2. As long as they define the steps correctly with all the runtime informations, they can smoothly switch between these servers/services with only a change of `--queue` (globally) or `on_host` (each step).

@AdriaanRol In the new parameter, a get_cmd and set_cmd can be passed. These are used if the Parameter does not have an explicit get/set method, and can be set to four things:
- `False`: The Parameter cannot be set if `set_cmd=None` (same for get)
- `None`: The parameter will store to (`set_cmd=None`) or load from (`get_cmd=None`) an attribute. If both `set_cmd` and `get_cmd` are set to None, this would correspond to the ManualParameter
- string: Used as visa command, like its current use in the StandardParameter
- callable: called upon get/set, like its current use in the StandardParameter.

For the parameter, the default kwargs are: `get_cmd=None`, and `set_cmd=False`. For the deprecated ManualParameter and StandardParameter, these are different.

By being able to set the `get/set_cmd` to any of these independently, Parameters can also be created that are part `ManualParameter` and part `StandardParameter`. One thing we ran into a few times is an Instrument whose VISA parameters are settable, but not gettable. In some cases we'd want the `get` method to return the latest set value. This wasn't easily doable before, but is now.

@xnox
At this point, not all functions of the HMC GUI are available at the HMC Web Services API. The GUI tasks are not going through the HMC API, they take an internal path, and therefore it is possible that a GUI task is not externalized at the HMC API.

Specifically, the "Load From Removable Media or Server" GUI task is not currently available at the HMC API. We are discussing the request to add support for it.

One alternative would be to use DPM mode (Dynamic Partition Manager). In that mode, a lot more dynamic capabilities are available, e.g. for creating partitions and devices on these partitions. DPM mode can be set for each CPC individually and requires a restart of the CPC. Once the CPC is in DPM mode, the partition can be created such that it boots from an FTP server, by setting the "boot-ftp-host" and related properties in the "Create Partition" operation at the HMC API.

Enabling DPM mode as some prerequisites:
* HMC and SE must be on V2.13.1 (2.13.0 is not sufficient)
* The CPC in question must be at the firmware level corresponding to SE 2.13.1.
* The following features must be enabled on the CPC:
  - Feature code 0016: "Hardware for DPM" feature
  - 2 times feature code 0417: "OSA-Express 1000BASE-T Ethernet" feature (for primary and backup connectivity)

The HMC API book (referenced in the zhmcclient references section) is supposed to be complete as far as the functionality of the HMC API is concerned. Also, the request parameters are supposed to be properly documented in the HMC API book. If you find that any of that is not the case, please let us know (e.g. by opening an issue on the zhmcclient project; we'll pass it on to the authors of the book as a defect, as long as its not hundreds of them ;-)

Using the /api/console/tasks/... URI for this task is not going to work, because these URIs are used only when it comes to authorizing users for tasks (e.g. via the "Add Permission to User Role" operation). These URIs cannot be used to invoke these tasks.

There seems to be a different behavior between Python 2.7 and Python 3 when it comes to testing warnings (even though the documentation looks identical between the two versions: https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/warnings.html#testing-warnings).

Python 2 requires to manually clear the warning registry if we want to be able to catch them:

```python
import inspect
module = inspect.getmodule(Dropdown)
getattr(module, '__warningregistry__', {}).clear()
```

This has also been mentioned in other project testing for warning being triggered, for example with Matplotlib: https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/commit/0017d3e19f4619cb9a79f7432b17b02c4ebd315b

We namespace most of the phosphor css in case a different version of phosphor is on the page - our embedded bundle's phosphor would then stomp on the global phosphor's css.

However, we now make an exception for the top-level `.p-widget` css, since it is causing problems with specificity in our widget css selectors (i.e., overriding things it shouldn't). We could say that it is up to the widget manager to put this one snippet on the page, but for backwards compatibility, we just do it still. This has the possibility of stomping on possibly different phosphor css already on the page, but I think that conflict unlikely as the `.p-widget` css is very minimal.

I agree with @Madhu94 about taking a single list of widgets and transparently exposing the CSS grid attributes.

The DOM structure of the frontend widget is explicitly an implementation detail, so it is not exposed to the user as an API. However:

1. we do have a flexible framework for adding such styles to the various style attributes on widgets (for example, we recently added a style attribute to buttons that lets you set the ToggleButtons font weight with something `ToggleButtons(options=['a','b','c'], style={'font_weight': 'bold'})`

2. For systematic changes across all widgets, a theme setting is probably better. The Jupyter notebook isn't designed for theming, but JupyterLab is, and there it is much easier to change the system-wide font size, which would also affect ipywidgets.

Major issues have now been resolved as far as I can tell.


## re Minor.2: ingest `location` parameter

Ingest is very much file based NetCDF producing app, it's a miracle it can work at all with the new plugin infrastructure. Proper support for ingesting to non-file locations needs more thought. Can be addressed after merge.


## re Minor.3: default environment name

Yes it's an error, but it's an error that enables backwards compatibility with
previous `[datacube]` config. This should be addressed in a separate PR.

## re Minor.4: lazy loading drivers

- Users have an option of not installing drivers they won't care about using
- Plugin driver writers already have an option of lazy initialization internally
- Driver cache is itself lazy loaded, so things like `datacube --help` should not need to pay the cost of loading all the drivers
- If you don't write data, writer drivers should not be loaded


## re Minor 5 and 6

Can wait til after merge.

Well, there's *public* and "public". We can probably do without the top-level scan and verify methods, they're mostly for circumventing problems you really should fix by using a different child-watcher class in the first place.

The most scratchy part I see is the choice of sync vs. async watcher and the fact that there are different ways of using them. I suspect that that can't really be helped even when we add some other ways of dealing with child processes. The best way to do it under Linux that I can think of, other than threads which have their own messy trade-offs, is by using a signalfd on SIGCHLD, but that still requires most of the scaffolding I added for dealing with SIGCHLD directly.

I don't see the internals of the watcher classes as public interface per se. You don't actually use any of that; all you do is `await wait_for_child()` and you're done. All that's really public on part of the handlers are their class names.

In fact, we should hide the whole mess (adding Windows will not make it look any nicer) behind the `child_watcher()` call. With a bit of experience what works when, it should make a reasonable choice. Shall I move the detail stuff into a couple of trio/_wait/*.py files so that `child_watcher()` can selectively import the one it needs?

Hi @vladimiriacob,

This is actually a weird behavior in python-gitlab, something we need to fix.

The workaround is to create a ``ProjectJob`` lazy object using the ``ProjectPipelineJob`` you created (untested but should work):

```
pipeline = project.pipelines.create({'ref': ref})
jobs = pipeline.jobs.list()
project.jobs.get(jobs[0].id, lazy=True).play()
```

The ``lazy`` attribute avoids an extra API call, but will create an object that properly supports the ``play()`` method.

@liwchang @linkerzhang 
1. I think I get the “Functions” proposal (#802). It lets users define sequences of nodes that will operate on given inputs, an return resulting outputs. This indeed would allow wrapping the sequence that will be equivalent to a compound node that would need zero points I had in mind. Appreciate your confirmation.
Let’s look at the required sequence however:
(A - zpA) * (B – zpB) = A*B – zpA * SumColumns(B) – zpB * SumRows(A) + zpA * zpB * CommonDimension(A,B)
The first term would map to MatMul_Integer. The last term would map to broadcasted Add (Tensor,Scalar).
The mid terms should map to  broadcasted Mul(zpA, ReduceSum(B, axis=1) and Mul(zpB, ReduceSum(A, axis=0).
In order to be able to execute mid terms, we need to have the ReduceSum() operator to receive inputs of (int/uint)(8/16) and return int32 result. Can we add this extension as a part of this proposal?

2a. If you subtract zero_point from the quantized tensor, it will overflow for sure. Let me give you a concrete example. Let’s assume we have a tensor with real values from 0.0 to 63.75. When we quantize it to int8 with zero_point, the scale will be (63.75-0)/ (127 - -128) = 0.25 and zero_point will be -128.
0.0f is clearly in the dynamic range. Now if I take a positive int8 number from the quantized tensor, say, 3, and subtract the zero_point from it, I get 3 – (-128) = 131 which is not representable by int8. The only case this math will not overflow is when the zero_point is 0, which defeats the purpose of the zero_point. The suggested method of casting to int32 will not let me do GEMM in int8, which is where the efficiency comes from. Therefore, I am actually forced to use the decomposition in my (1).
2b. Your proposal already stipulates that zero_point must be of the same type as input tensor for the reasons of padding. This makes this kind of hypothetical, amn’t I wrong here?

Now to a different point. In order to be able to implement LinearQuantize “function” from existing operators, we need to be able to perform rounding. Currently, ONNX spec contains only the following rounding-related operators: Floor, Ceil. Using these operators, it is only possible to implement the following rounding policies: round-ties-up and round-ties-down (via Floor(Tensor + 0.5), Ceil(Tensor -0.5)). Are we OK to limit ourselves to those rounding methods, or do we want to introduce Round operator which will round ties away from zero, or NearbyInt that lets user chose their preferred rounding method?

@phdowling 
>Actually, I also just came across this: nmslib/hnsw#2 - is this the same in nmslib, or is the dynamic updating only supported by the implementation in that repository?

It is a reimplementation, optimized for less memory usage, faster construction and incremental indexing. 
Nmslib's c++ interface also supports incremental insertions for sw-graph and hnsw (only regular indexes).
If you only need regular dense spaces with incremental updates I would advice using hnswlib.

Hm. Your system NumPy v1.1.x (is this correct?) is too early for ParaView and PVGeo, ParaView needs at least v1.8.1 and PVGeo needs at least v1.10.x

To mitigate this, the NumPy installed alongside PVGeo needs to be linked to ParaView, not the system NumPy.

From the environment that you installed PVGeo via pip, could you activate python and find the path to NumPy? I recommend ensuring PVGeo is nestled in its own conda env but it appears yours are simply under conda's global site-packages?

```py
>>> import PVGeo # Make sure we are in the correct environment and that it imports
>>> import numpy
>>> numpy.__version__ # check that version is greater than v1.10.x
'1.14.1'
>>> numpy.__file__
'some/path/to/this/environments/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py'
```
If this path (up to numpy) is different than what you previous tried linking to ParaView, could you try linking this numpy?
